coredata.dc:title: Dual-mode colorimetric/fluorometric sensor for the detection of glutathione based on the peroxidase-like activity of carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Guan, Huanan
Wang, Dandan
Sun, Bingyu
coredata.dc:description: In the current investigation, fluorescent carbon quantum dot nanoparticles were prepared using hydrothermal method. In order to achieve green preparation, waste corncob was used as raw material. Transmission electron microscope was used to examine the urface morphology of the synthesized fluorescent carbon quantum dot nanoparticles. Optical characterization was done by analyzing the ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy and fluorescence absorption spectrum. The peroxidase activity and catalytic performance of corncob fluorescent carbon quantum dots have been researched and compared with some simulated enzymes, and corncob fluorescent carbon quantum dots with peroxisase-like activity and fluorescence characteristics were used for the detection of glutathione by dual-mode colorimetric/fluorometric sensor. Our experimental results displayed that the corncob fluorescent carbon quantum dots have nanostructures and the catalytic activity of corncob fluorescent carbon quantum dots was better than that of other nanoparticles. The detection results of glutathione show that the limit of detection was 8.0133 nmol/L for the fluorometric sensor and the limit of detection was 0.3024 μmol/L for the colorimetric sensor with good selectivity and high sensitivity, exhibiting their potential for application in nanosensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots
Mimic enzyme
Colorimetric sensor
Fluorescent sensor
Glutathione
Food analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of waste corncob fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CFCD)
Visualization analysis of the CFCD simulated enzyme activity
Analysis of glutathione detection by the fluorometric sensor
Analysis of glutathione detection by colorimetric sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Major Science and Technology Special Projects of Heilongjiang Province (2019ZX08B02), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31201376 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province of China (No. C2016034 ), the “ Young Innovative Talent ” Support Program (No. 2019CX05 ), the Heilongjiang Postdoctoral Research Launch Project (No. LBH-Q19027 ), the Heilongjiang Leading Talent Support Program (No. 2020376 ) and the Harbin Business University Graduate Research Innovation Project (No. HSD20210705 )

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CFCD with the scale bar 500 nm. Inset: TEM images of CFCD with the scale bar 100 nm.

UV absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the corncob fluorescent carbon quantum dots. (A: UV absorption spectra of the corncob fluorescent carbon quantum dots; B: Fluorescence spectra of the corncob fluorescent carbon quantum dots.)
FT-IR spectra of the corncob fluorescent carbon quantum dots.

UV–vis absorption spectra of the peroxidase-like activity of CFCD. CFCD/H2O2 system (curve a), CFCD-ABTS system (curve b), H2O2-ABTS system (curve c), CFCD-H2O2-ABTS system (curve d). Inset: Images of color reaction in different systems.

Steady-state kinetic assay. The concentration of ABTS was 10 mmol/L and the H2O2 concentration was varied (A). The concentration of H2O2 was 50 mmol/L and the ABTS concentration was varied (B). Double reciprocal plots of activity of GoldMag with the concentration of one substrate (ABTS or H2O2) fixed and the other varied (C, D).
Effect of H2O2 concentration (A), temperature (B), CFCD addition (C) on the fluorimetric method for GSH detection.

A response curve for GSH detection using fluorimetric method. Inset: The linear relationship between ΔFL with the GSH concentrations in the range from 0.05 mmol/L to 10 mmol/L. Error bars represent the standard derivation (SD) of replicates measurements with n = 3.

Selectivity of the assay for GSH compared to other potential interferences. The concentration of GSH is 1 mmol/L, other interferences concentration are 100 mmol/L. Error bars represent the standard derivation (SD) of replicates measurements with n = 3.
Effect of H2O2 concentration (A), temperature (B), CFCD addition (C) on the colorimetric method for GSH detection.

A response curve for GSH detection using colorimetric method. Inset: The linear relationship between ΔA with the GSH concentrations in the range from 0.005 mmol/L to 1 mmol/L. Error bars represent the standard derivation (SD) of replicates measurements with n = 3.

Selectivity of the assay for GSH compared to other potential interferences. The concentration of GSH is 0.1 mmol/L, other interferences concentration are 10 mmol/L. Error bars represent the standard derivation (SD) of replicates measurements with n = 3.
Analysis of GSH concentration in the real samples based on fluorometric sensor.
Analysis of GSH concentration in the real samples based on colorimetric sensor.

head.ce:title: Dual-mode colorimetric/fluorometric sensor for the detection of glutathione based on the peroxidase-like activity of carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Engineering, Harbin University of Commerce, Harbin 150076, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Engineering, Harbin University of Commerce, Harbin 150076, People’s Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Harbin University of Commerce, Harbin, Heilongjiang Province 150076, People’s Republic of China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the current investigation, fluorescent carbon quantum dot nanoparticles were prepared using hydrothermal method. In order to achieve green preparation, waste corncob was used as raw material. Transmission electron microscope was used to examine the urface morphology of the synthesized fluorescent carbon quantum dot nanoparticles. Optical characterization was done by analyzing the ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy and fluorescence absorption spectrum. The peroxidase activity and catalytic performance of corncob fluorescent carbon quantum dots have been researched and compared with some simulated enzymes, and corncob fluorescent carbon quantum dots with peroxisase-like activity and fluorescence characteristics were used for the detection of glutathione by dual-mode colorimetric/fluorometric sensor. Our experimental results displayed that the corncob fluorescent carbon quantum dots have nanostructures and the catalytic activity of corncob fluorescent carbon quantum dots was better than that of other nanoparticles. The detection results of glutathione show that the limit of detection was 8.0133 nmol/L for the fluorometric sensor and the limit of detection was 0.3024 μmol/L for the colorimetric sensor with good selectivity and high sensitivity, exhibiting their potential for application in nanosensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH), a natural-synthetic peptide in the human cytoplasm, including glutamate, cysteine and glycine, widely distributed in the body organs, plays a vital role in maintaining cell biological function, it can activate a variety of enzymes, thus promote sugar, fat and protein metabolism, and can affect the cell metabolic process [1–4]. GSH provides a variety of cellular activities in biological systems, it can be combined with the free radicals in the body through sulfur groups and can be transformed into acids quickly metabolized, thus accelerating the excretion of free radicals, helping to reduce the toxic side effects of chemotherapy and radiotherapy, and has no noticeable effect on the curative effect of chemotherapy and radiotherapy. In addition, GSH can protect liver synthesis, detoxification, inactivation hormones and other functions to promote bile acid metabolism and facilitate the absorption of fat and fat-soluble vitamins in the digestive tract [2–5]. Meanwhile, the level of GSH in tissues has been linked to various of disorders in children, including HIV, AIDS, cancer, Parkinson's, and autism [3–4]. GSH has also been widely employed in health foods [5], cosmetics industries [6], and pharmaceuticals [7] due to its crucial physiological qualities, such as as an antioxidant and detoxifier. Thus, it is crucial to develop GSH detection techniques with high selectivity and sensitivity. Nowadays, many methods are used to detect GSH, such as high performance liquid chromatography [8], electrochemiluminescence [9], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [10], electrochemical [11], capillary electrophoresis [12], enzyme-linked immunosorbent method [13] et al. Although these strategies show promising results in GSH detection, they also have some drawbacks, such as time consumption, requiring skilled technicians and so on. Therefore, it is essential to develop suitable probes to detect GSH.

Natural active enzymes are widely used in many fields, especially colorimetric sensors, because of their high catalytic efficiency, stability, and specificity [14,15]. However, various limiting considerations, such as high cost, difficulty to preserve, severely limit their utilization. Therefore, it is essential to develop simulated enzymes with the high catalytic activity of natural enzymes and overcome the shortcomings of natural enzymes. Nano mimetic enzyme overcomes many defects such as cumbersome preparation of natural enzyme, high denaturation rate under harsh conditions, high cost of purification and preservation and has the advantages of low toxicity, easy preparation, high stability and good biocompatibility. In the study carried out by Mei et al. [16], a highly dispersed nano-enzyme based on the assembly of natural glucose oxidase (GOD) onto CoFe-layered double hydroxides (CoFe-LDHs) monolayer nanosheets, which applied for clinical cancer treatment. Joshi et al. [17] developed a biocompatible, economically competitive, reduced graphene oxide (rGO) film. The film was obtained from shellac using a heat treatment (TrGO). After the heat treatment, the rGO (TrGO) film showed good crystallinity, low foil resistance, and high carbon content. From the TrGO, electrochemical immunosensors were produced without labels for the quantitative detection of the H1N1 influenza virus employing electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. In the study carried out by Lin et al. [18], FeO@C nanoparticles functionalized with amine were used as magnetic carriers for laccase immobilization by adsorption. As a result, the operational, pH, and storage stability of the immobilized laccase were significantly improved, and after 10 consecutive operations, it maintained its residual activity above 60%. Therefore, it has become the most potential nanomaterial in the field of mimetic enzymes in recent years. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted much attention because of their excellent chemical stability compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots [19], good photobleaching resistance compared with organic dyes [20], low toxicity compared with precious metal nanoclusters [21], good biocompatibility [22], environmental friendliness [23]. As a novel nano mimetic enzyme, fluorescent carbon quantum dots have predictable reactivity, tunable catalytic activity, and good stability. They are employed in a variety of applications, including food [24], biology [25], medicine [26], and the environment [27].

Recently, CQDs prepared using by corncob as a carbon source have been widely used. Xie et al. developed a novel and environmental-friendly photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs decorated by corncob biomass carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was fabricated successfully via photoinduced method for the first time [28]. Combined with the rational utilization of corncob waste biomass, this research indicates green photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs synthesized by photoinduced method will have wide potential in water pollutant treatment in the future. In the study carried out by Zhang et al., the CQDs were synthesized using by corncob residues (CRs) obtained after hemicelluloses pre-extraction of corncobs, and the enzymatically hydrolyzed corncob residues (EHCRs), which successfully applied to the concentration of iron (III) in human blood [29]. Herein, waste corncob fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CFCD) were green prepared by one-step hydrothermal method with corncob as raw material. Their characterization, peroxidase activity verification, and enzymatic reaction kinetics were studied. CFCD, as a mimic enzyme, can catalyze the oxidation of H2O2 to create ·OH, ·OH quenching the fluorescence of CFCD, which is restored when GSH is introduced. CFCD has peroxidase-like activity that can catalyze the oxidation of peroxidase substrate to oxidize colorless ABTS to green ox-ABTS. Glutathione is a kind of reductive antioxidant, which can scavenge ABTS radicals and reduce green ABTS·+ to colorless ABTS2−, resulting in noticeable green fading. Based on this, the dual-mode colorimetric/fluorometric sensor for the detection of glutathione was realized. In addition, the detection of glutathione based on dual-mode colorimetric/fluorometric sensor can effectively avoid the interferences from biological molecules. Moreover, the highly selective, cost-effective fluorescence and colorimetric dual-readout assay has also been successfully applied to the detection of glutathione in complicated real samples, possessing more potential in biological fields.

Corncob was produced in Harbin Xiangfang farm, China. Glacial acetic acid (HAc), acetic acid sodium salt trihydrate (NaAc·3H2O), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 3%), 2,2′-amino-di (2-ethyl-benzothiazoline sulphonic acid-6) ammonium salt (ABTS) and glutathione, were obtained from Sladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the reagents were of analytical grade and required no further purification. All those used in this experiment were deionized water.

UV–vis spectra were recorded on a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Beijing Shinetek Instruments Co., Ltd., China). The fluorescence spectra were collected using the F97 Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai Lingguang Technology Co., Ltd., China). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were performed on an HT-7700 TEM (JEOL, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of waste corncob fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CFCD)
ce:section.ce:para: Waste corncobs were cut into 3 cm pieces, crushed to 60 mesh with a crusher, and then the corncob powder was dried at 60 °C for 48 h and sealed for standby. Corncob powder (0.6 g) was dissolved in 30 mL deionized water and stirred for 30 min, the obtained solution was transferred into a teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave which was sealed and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. The autoclave was then allowed to cool to room temperature naturally. Centrifuge the obtained black reaction solution at 3000 r/min for 5 min, and filter it with the microporous membrane (Φ 0.45 μm). The CFCD suspension is obtained. CFCD suspension was dried at 60 °C, washed twice with deionized water, then dried, and the CFCD powder was kept at 4 °C for reserve.

In a certain concentration range, by changing the ABTS and H2O2, the enzyme kinetics of CFCD was evaluated by the steady-state kinetic method, and the double reciprocal curve was constructed to calculate the kinetic reaction parameters. The reaction temperature was 50 °C, added 150 μL CFCD (2 mg/mL) in 3 mL NaAc buffer (pH 4.0). When the H2O2 concentrations were fixed for enzymatic kinetic analysis, different concentrations of ABTS were added to 500 μL H2O2 (50 mmol/L) buffer. When ABTS concentrations were fixed to enzymatic kinetic analysis, ABTS (10 mmol/L) was added to different concentrations of H2O2 buffer. The UV–Vis spectrophotometer was employed for time-driven scanning within a specific substrate concentration range, and the absorbance of the reaction system was measured every 30 s at 420 nm. The Michaelis-Menten constant is calculated by using the Lineweaver-Burk graph of the dual reciprocal Michaelis-Menten equation. The Michaelis-Menten constant is 1/v = K m/V m (1/[S] + 1/K m), where v is the initial velocity, V m is the maximum reaction velocity, [S] corresponds to the substrate concentration, and K m is Michaelis constant [30].

The fluorescent detection of GSH was carried out as followed: the optimum volume of CFCD (2.0 mg/mL), the optimal concentration of H2O2, 3 mL HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0) were mixed uniformly and incubated for 10 min at optimal temperature, then the fluorescence intensity (λex = 500 nm) was recorded, record as F0 , and different concentrations of GSH (150 L each) were added into the CFCD sensor solution, the fluorescent spectra (λex = 500 nm) were recorded after 3 min, record as F. In order to obtain the best detection system, a single factor optimization test was carried out. The reaction conditions for the novel fluorescence system were optimized using ΔFL Intensity (ΔFL Intensity = F0 -F), where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity at 475 nm in the absence and presence of GSH. Under the optimum conditions, the relationship between ΔFL Intensity and GSH concentration. Similarly, the selectivity for GSH determination was tested by substituting various interfering compounds for GSH. In a similar manner, the anti-interference experiment was carried out by concurrently adding GSH and interfering chemicals.

The colorimetric detection of GSH was carried out as followed: 150 μL CFCD (2.0 mg/mL), appropriate H2O2 concentration, 200 μL ABTS (5.0 mmol/L), 3 mL HAc-NaAc buffer, (pH 4.0) were uniformly mixed and then incubated for 10 min at optimal temperature. At this point, a colorimetric probe was generated to detect GSH, which was the green CFCD-ABTS free radical system, and its absorbance was measured at 420 nm, denoted as A0 . In the CFCD-ABTS system, 100 μL GSH with A concentration of 1–100 mmol/L was added. After the appropriate reaction time, the absorbance was measured at 420 nm, denoted as A. In order to obtain the best detection system, a single factor optimization test was carried out. The reaction conditions for the novel Colorimetric system were optimized using ΔA (ΔA = A0 − A), where A0 and A are the absorbance at 420 nm in the absence and presence of GSH. Under the optimum conditions, the relationship between ΔA and GSH concentration. The selectivity test and anti-interference test is the same as the fluorescence method.

1 g flour was fully dissolved by 30 mL 1% dilute sulfuric acid, then shook the dissolving solution well and centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 5 min. The supernatant was taken for later use. GSH in flour was detected by the above two detection methods.

The CFCD used in this experiment is obtained by the one-step water thermal method. The size and morphology of the CFCD were observed by TEM (Fig. 1 ). The images reveal that the CFCD has a prominent nanostructure and good divergence with no apparent aggregation. The illustrations show CFCD as spherical particles with a smooth surface and particle size less than 10 nm. It indicates that CFCD nanoparticles were successfully prepared.

The optical properties of CFCD were explored by UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum, as shown in Fig. 2 . Absorption spectral analysis is the most common technique used to confirm the formation of the nanoparticles. The absorption spectra of pure CFCD nanoparticles is shown in Fig. 2A, respectively. The absorption peak for the pure CFCD nanoparticles is observed at 324 nm. It is mainly attributed to capturing of the exciting energy by the surface states and typically exhibits blue fluorescence [31]. It is presumed that the CFCD may be based on the surface defect state luminous. The fluorescence spectrum of CFCD, as shown in Fig. 2B, shows a distinct fluorescence emission peak around 475 nm, indicating that CFCD has a more p-p conjugated double bond molecular structure [32].

Fig. 3 shows the FT-IR spectrum of CFCD. As shown in Fig. 3, the hydroxyl stretching and bending vibrations of CFCD correspond to the absorption peaks at 3327 cm−1 and 1492 cm−1, indicating that the CFCD surface contains rich hydroxyl groups, ensuring good hydrophilic and stability.The presence of CO telescopic vibration peak at around 1600 cm−1 indicates that the prepared CFCD surface contains hydrophilic groups, such as carboxyl groups, etc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization analysis of the CFCD simulated enzyme activity
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like activity of CFCD was assessed by catalytic oxidation of peroxidase substrate ABTS in the presence of H2O2 to study its peroxidase-like activity. As shown in Fig. 4 , the CFCD-H2O2 (curve a) and CFCD-ABTS (curve b) were colorless. Moreover, there is no absorption peak for H2O2-ABTS solution without CFCD in buffer (curve c). Meanwhile, the system of CFCD-H2O2-ABTS exhibited deep green (curve d). The CFCD-H2O2 system had no prominent absorption peaks indicating no catalytic reactions between CFCD and H2O2. The CFCD-ABTS system and the H2O2-ABTS system both exhibited no obvious absorption at 420 nm, suggesting that CFCD had no catalyzation to ABTS, while the reaction of the H2O2-ABTS system had no distinguishable change without any catalysts, indicating that CFCD oxidized ABTS to ABTS·+. The CFCD-H2O2-ABTS system has a prominent absorption peak at wavelength 420 nm could be observed, indicating that CFCD has peroxidase-like enzymatic activity.

The catalytic activity of CFCD was investigated utilizing enzyme kinetics study with H2O2 and ABTS as substrates in order to investigate the enzyme kinetics of the peroxidase catalytic activity of CFCD. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the reaction rates increased linearly after increasing H2O2 concentration using ABTS as a substrate and then grew slowly until stayed steady. With H2O2 as the substrate, the influence of ABTS concentration on the reaction rate was also investigated (Fig. 5B). The reaction rates demonstrated an analogical propensity to have a linear relationship with ABTS at acceptable concentrations until they reached their maximum. The apparent kinetic parameters for ABTS catalytic oxidation in the appropriate range of H2O2 and ABTS concentrations were calculated. The following Beer Lambert's law was used to calculate the initial reaction rate.
c = A / ε × b
while b is the thickness of the solution, A is the absorbance value, and c represents the substrate concentration. Where ε = 36,000 M−1cm−1 for the oxidized product of ABTS at 420 nm and V = dc/dt. Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) was obtained using the Lineweaver-Burk plot. The lower the value of K m, the higher the enzyme-substrate affinity. Table 1 summarizes the findings. Table 1 also included the apparent kinetic parameters of HRP and CFCD. The Km value was lower than HRP's with both H2O2 and ABTS as the substrate. Furthermore, it suggests that CFCD has a stronger affinity for ABTS than HRP. In this test, the Km value of H2O2 as a substrate is as low as 1.1318 mmol/L, indicating that CFCD has a high affinity for H2O2 substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of glutathione detection by the fluorometric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 6 , the effect of H2O2 concentration, reaction temperature and the addition of CFCD on the detection system were investigated. The relative activity of the assay system has the optimal detection peak at the H2O2 concentration of 10 mmol/L, as shown in Fig. 6A. The effect of the reaction temperature from 30 to 70 °C on the detection system is shown in Fig. 6B, as the reaction temperature was raised, the relative activity increased at first, then declined. The degree of fluorescence quenching is greatest when the temperature reaches 50 °C. As the result, the optimal temperature for the following test was 50 °C. As shown in Fig. 6C, the effect of CFCD addition to the detection system ranged from 50 to 200 μL. The addition amount of CFCD of 150 μL with possesses rapid response-ability for GSH detection. In further experiments, the CFCD addition of 150 μL was selected for GSH detection to obtain the best sensitivity. The results suggested that the CFCD fluorescence quenching system-based sensing platform for GSH detection possessed fast response-ability and good stability.

Under the optimum conditions, the relationship between ΔFL Intensity and GSH concentration in the range of 0.05–150 mmol/L was detected, the ΔFL Intensity was proportional to the concentration of GSH in the two linear ranges from 0.05 mmol/L to 10 mmol/L (Fig. 7 C) and 25 mmol/L to 150 mmol/L (Fig. 7D). The linear regression equations were fitted as y = 30.6990x + 76.7681 with the correlation coefficient of 0.9952 and y = 1.0309x + 485.0121 with the correlation coefficient of 0.9674. In addition, the limit of detection (LOD) was 8.0133 nmol/L with a signal/noise (S/N) ratio of 3. It is well-known that the peroxidase-like performance of the nanomaterials is attributed to the generation of the reactive oxygen species (ROS) via the decomposition of H2O2 by the catalyst. In general, the hydroxide (
OH) and superoxide (O2
−) radicals are typical ROS during the nanomaterials-based peroxidase-like reaction. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of the system gradually increased with the GSH concentration. CFCD can effectively catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into ·OH, thus quenching the CFCD auto-fluorescence, while GSH can restore ·OH to H2O2 to restore the system fluorescence. It proposed that the attractive interaction between CFCD is the driving force of H2O2 binding to the active site, and that this H2O2 adsorption promotes the production of
OH radicals during the catalytic oxidation. Therefore, it is very likely that a similar H2O2 binding and
OH radical generation mechanism operated at the active sites of CFCD, so that K m H 2 O 2 increased with H2O2 concentration. That is to say, the as-prepared CFCD were capable of decomposing H2O2 into
OH by absorbing H2O2 on the surface of the nanocomposites.

Solvent-induced energy transfer theory is the leading cause of fluorescence enhancement, namely the luminescence emission intensity as the electronic π-conjugated structure increases and the electron density of the compound increases [38]. The recovery of GSH standard solution (1, 5, 10 mmol/L) was evaluated by the standard addition method in the experiment. The results show that the recovery rate of this fluorescence detection system is in the range of 96.72% to 105.90%. The results show that the proposed sensing system has high accuracy, good stability and reproducibility.

The impacts of some representative interfering compounds potentially coexisting in actual sample on determining GSH, including various amino acids and metal ions (Thr, Try, Glu, Gly, L-Cys, K+ and Na+), were explored to investigate the sensor's selectivity toward GSH. As shown in Fig. 8 , Thr, Try, Glu, Gly had a very negligible effect on the detection system, and only L-Cys exhibited slight interference for fluorescent detecting GSH. The K+ and Na+ were weakly bound to CFCD or are unable to form a stable complex because of their high aqueous solubility. Therefore, the K+ and Na+ do not significantly affect the FL Intensity of hydroxy-terminated CFCD. The CFCD system increases in fluorescence intensity after adding GSH, which cannot be altered in the presence of other species. CFCD can effectively catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into hydroxyl radicals, thus quenching the CFCD autofluorescence, while GSH can restore hydroxyl radicals to H2O2 to restore the system fluorescence while it had a relatively more substantial reduction than other distractors. In conclusion, the CFCD mimic enzyme fluorescence sensing system has good selectivity for detecting GSH.

To evaluate the detection performance of the CFCD fluorescence sensing system for GSH in actual food samples, the ability of the concentration in flour, and the reliability of the actual sample. Recovery assays for GSH by adding different known amounts of GSH standard solution to the flour, the results are presented in Table 2 , the average recovery was between 96.70%∼100.73% and relative standard deviation (RSD) was below 3.48%. According to the experimental results, the proposed sensing system can be applied to the rapid analysis of practical samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of glutathione detection by colorimetric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In the presence of H2O2, CFCD shows better peroxidase activity and could catalyze the oxidation of ABTS to produce green-colored ox-ABTS. Glutathione is a powerful antioxidant that scavenges ABTS radicals and causes the conversion of green ABTS·+ to colorless ABTS2−, resulting in the considerable green color fade. As a result, GSH could be detected by colorimetric sensor.

As shown in Fig. 9 , the effect of H2O2 concentration, reaction temperature and the addition of CFCD on the detection system were investigated. The effect of the H2O2 concentration from 1 to 50mmoL/L on the detection system is shown in Fig. 9A, the relative activity of the assay system has the optimal detection peak at the H2O2 concentration of 50 mmol/L. Since excess H2O2 could convert HRP into an inactive form and inhibit its catalytically active [39], 50 mmol/L of H2O2 was selected for subsequent assays. Thankfully, in our trial, the CFCD catalytic activity was well-established stable at the H2O2 concentration of 50 mmol/L. However, excess H2O2 reacts with GSH with an effect on the CFCD-ABTS radical clearance system, thus affecting the experimental accuracy and sensitivity. Thus, 50 mmol/L of H2O2 was selected for subsequent assays. The effect of the reaction temperature from 30 to 70 °C on the detection system is shown in Fig. 9B. With the reaction temperature was raised, the relative activity increased. It demonstrates that in a given temperature range, the higher the temperature, the better the catalysis of the CFCD peroxidase. Given the utility of this sensing system, the high reaction temperature is not conducive to its field analysis and rapid detection. Therefore, the temperature of 70 °C is the optimal condition for subsequent testing. As shown in Fig. 9C, the effect of CFCD addition to the detection system ranged from 50 to 200 μL. The addition amount of CFCD of 150 μL possesses rapid response-ability for GSH detection. In further experiments, the CFCD addition of 150 μL was selected for GSH detection to obtain the best sensitivity. The results show that the GSH detection colorimetric sensing platform based on the CFCD-ABTS radical clearing system has rapid responsiveness and good stability.

Under the optimum conditions, the relationship between ΔA and GSH concentration in the range of 0.005–5 mmol/L was detected, the ΔA was proportional to the concentration of GSH in the two linear ranges from 0.005 mmol/L to 1 mmol/L (Fig. 10 C) and 1 mmol/L to 5 mmol/L (Fig. 10D). The linear regression equations were fitted as y = 0.8141x + 0.1612 with the correlation coefficient of 0.9932 and y = 0.0459x + 0.9071 with the correlation coefficient of 0.9837. In addition, the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.3024 μmol/L with a signal/noise (S/N) ratio of 3. The recovery of GSH standard solution (0.1, 0.5, 1 mmol/L) was evaluated by the standard addition method in the experiment. The results show that the recovery rate of this colorimetric detection system is in the range of 97.06% to 105.72%. The results show that the proposed sensing system has high accuracy, good stability and reproducibility.

The impacts of some representative interfering compounds potentially coexisting in an actual sample on determining GSH, including KCl, NaCl, Gly, Thr, Leu, Suc and Fru, were explored to investigate the sensor's selectivity toward GSH. As shown in Fig. 11 , ΔA was assessed as a standard and showed significant changes in the presence of GSH. However, increasing the interfering substance concentration as 100-fold that of GSH concentration could not significantly change absorbance. GSH can restore green ABTS·+ to colorless ABTS2−, while other interfering substances have little effect on the CFCD-ABTS system. In conclusion, the colorimetric-based CFCD-ABTS system has good selectivity for GSH detection.

To evaluate the detection performance of the CFCD colorimetric sensing system for GSH in actual food samples, the ability of the concentration in flour, and the reliability of the actual sample. Recovery assays for measuring GSH by adding different known amounts of GSH standard solution to the flour, the results are presented in Table 3 , the average recovery between 99.80%∼102.00% and relative standard deviation (RSD) below 3.65%. According to the experimental results, the proposed sensing system can be applied to the rapid analysis of practical samples.

In summary, we successfully prepared a small particle size and good catalytic active CFCD. With one-step hydrothermal method using corncob as the carbon source. The GSH was examined using the colorimetric method based on the CFCD simulated peroxidase activity. Moreover, due to the unique fluorescence properties of CFCD, the fluorescence intensity changes simultaneously as a catalytic substance of the simulated enzyme, thus achieving the purpose of detecting GSH. The novel as-prepared CFCD promises fluorescent nanomaterials that show a great potential in the field of food analysis.

Huanan Guan: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Conceptualization, Supervision. Dandan Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation. Bingyu Sun: Software, Writing – original draft, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Major Science and Technology Special Projects of Heilongjiang Province (2019ZX08B02), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31201376), the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province of China (No. C2016034), the “Young Innovative Talent” Support Program (No. 2019CX05), the Heilongjiang Postdoctoral Research Launch Project (No. LBH-Q19027), the Heilongjiang Leading Talent Support Program (No. 2020376) and the Harbin Business University Graduate Research Innovation Project (No. HSD20210705)
